entry.title: A group theoretic proof of the infinitude of the primes

link.title: A group theoretic proof of the infinitude of the primes

entry.content: For contradiction suppose that the set of all primes is finite and let $p$ be the largest prime. Let $q$ be a prime factor $2^p-1$, i.e., \(2^p\equiv 1 \pmod{q}.\) This implies that $2$ has order $p$ in $G = \mathbb{Z}_q\setminus{0}$. By Lagrange’s theorem, $p | |G| = q-1$, which implies that $p\leq q-1 < q$.
entry.summary: For contradiction suppose that the set of all primes is finite and let $p$ be the largest prime. Let $q$ be a prime factor $2^p-1$, i.e., \(2^p\equiv 1 \pmod{q}.\) This implies that $2$ has order $p$ in $G = \mathbb{Z}_q\setminus{0}$. By Lagrange’s theorem, $p | |G| = q-1$, which implies that $p\leq q-1 < q$.
